coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for the selective and ultrasensitive detection of pesticide residues via the synergetic effects of copper nanoclusters and carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Ling
Lu, Jian
Luo, Mai
Yu, Hua
Chen, Xiaojia
Deng, Jiagang
Hou, Xiaotao
Hao, Erwei
Wei, Jinchao
Li, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Agricultural products, such as foodstuffs and herbal medicines, may be contaminated by pesticides. Therefore, developing sensitive methods for pesticide detection are urgently needed for the assurance of food safety. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system based on blue-emitted nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and red-emitted copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) complex was fabricated for pesticide detection. The selective detection of thiram and paraquat, two typical widely used pesticides, can be easily realized by changing the solvent environment. The detection limit can be reached as low as 7.49 nM and 3.03 nM, respectively. A good linear correlation was found over the concentration range from 10 to 500 nM for thiram and 5 to 100 nM for paraquat. More interestingly, fast and visual detection of thiram and paraquat can be achieved via smartphone-based colorimetric analysis, which has provided an effective implementation for on-site monitoring of the levels of pesticide residues in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensors
Pesticide residues
Visual detection
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection for thiram and paraquat
PL mechanism of the ratiometric nanoprobe
Ratiometric fluorescence assay for thiram and paraquat

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CuNCs (A) and N-CQDs (B).
The UV–Vis spectra of CuNCs and N-CQDs (A) and the excitation/emission spectra of CuNCs (B) as well as the excitation/emission spectra of N-CQDs (C).
The fluorescence spectra of CuNCs, N-CQDs and N-CQDs@CuNCs (A) and UV–Vis spectrum of Cu-NCs and the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CQDs (B).

The Fluorescence spectra of theN-CQDs@CuNCs probe in the presence of different concentration of thiram (A) and paraquat (C); The plots of fluorescence intensity ratio of I0/I versus the concentration of thiram (B) and paraquat (D). The Photographs of thiram and paraquat in different concentrations under UV light (E).

The fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) of the probe in the presence of various pesticides and ions upon excitation of 350 nm (A), and the concentrations of pesticides are all 250 nM. The concentrations of ions are all 12.5 μM (50-fold as the pesticides); The fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) of the probe in the presence of various pesticides and ions upon excitation at 370 nm (B), and the concentrations of pesticides are all 50 nM. The concentrations of ions are all 10 μM (200-fold as the pesticides).
Schematic representation for the ratiometric fluorescent sensor for pesticides detection using N-CQDs@CuNCs.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for the selective and ultrasensitive detection of pesticide residues via the synergetic effects of copper nanoclusters and carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Macau SAR 999078, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Macau SAR 999078, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Centre of Research on Functional Ingredients from Agricultural Residues, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Efficacy Study on Chinese Materia Medica, Guangxi University of Chinese Medicine, Nanning 530200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Centre of Research on Functional Ingredients from Agricultural Residues
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Efficacy Study on Chinese Materia Medica

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Centre of Research on Functional Ingredients from Agricultural Residues, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Efficacy Study on Chinese Materia Medica, Guangxi University of Chinese Medicine, Nanning 530200, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Agricultural products, such as foodstuffs and herbal medicines, may be contaminated by pesticides. Therefore, developing sensitive methods for pesticide detection are urgently needed for the assurance of food safety. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system based on blue-emitted nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and red-emitted copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) complex was fabricated for pesticide detection. The selective detection of thiram and paraquat, two typical widely used pesticides, can be easily realized by changing the solvent environment. The detection limit can be reached as low as 7.49 nM and 3.03 nM, respectively. A good linear correlation was found over the concentration range from 10 to 500 nM for thiram and 5 to 100 nM for paraquat. More interestingly, fast and visual detection of thiram and paraquat can be achieved via smartphone-based colorimetric analysis, which has provided an effective implementation for on-site monitoring of the levels of pesticide residues in food.

ce:section.ce:para: Food is a paramount necessity of human beings; however, the safety of food has always been an urgent global issue despite the accelerating process of economic globalization. Reported unsafe events in food safety occur one after another worldwide (Medina, Pereira, Silva, Perestrelo, & Câmara, 2019; Misiou & Koutsoumanis, 2021; Wang et al., 2021). Food safety is highly evaluated by governments worldwide since it is closely correlated with people's health and life safety, national quality and even the competitiveness of domestic agricultural products in the international market (Li et al., 2021; Qian, Ren, et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021). Therefore, the quality of food or other crops homologous to food needs strategic requirements to be ensured. The excess pesticide residues used in modern cultivation to increase the production of agricultural grains have hindered the quality and safety of food. Pesticide residue misusage can result in problems such as breathing difficulties, mankind organ failure, malformation and/or even fatal disease (Sharma, Aulakh, & Malik, 2003; Xing et al., 2020). Thiram (bis(dimethylthiocarbamoyl) disulfide) and paraquat are typical widely used pesticides that have been employed in crop production for weeding and insecticidal sterilization, thus inevitably being left behind and possibly causing physical damage. For animals, it has been confirmed that thiram can induce tibial dyschondrolplasia (TD) in chickens and affect notochord curvature of fish. For humans, exposure to thiram can lead to reproductive and nervous system dysfunction. Paraquat has been widely used since it was first synthesized in the 19th century. However, studies have shown that the pesticide affects dopaminergic neurons, which in turn increases the risk of neurological diseases such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's. In addition, people ingesting paraquat can cause pulmonary fibrosis, respiratory failure and inflammation, and the lack of effective drug treatment often leads to high mortality rates (Li, Liu, Kuang, & Xu, 2020; Pu, Huang, Xu, & Sun, 2021; Reczek et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019). It is of great significance to detect pesticide residues in food or other crops homologous to food.

Some methods, including liquid chromatography coupled to tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS), electrochemistry and Raman scattering techniques, are effective in detecting these contaminants (Botta et al., 2020; Maximiano, de Lima, Cardoso, & Arruda, 2018; Peruga, Grimalt, López, Sancho, & Hernández, 2012; Xing et al., 2020). Nevertheless, these precise instruments are time-consuming and complicated for their requirement of professional operator. Alternatively, fluorescent technology has made great progress in the field of rapid detection due to its fast interaction with analytes and reliable visible results (Luo et al., 2020; Oh et al., 2020; L. Yang et al., 2020). Fluorescent metal nanoclusters (gold nanoclusters, silver nanoclusters, etc.), which possess fascinating photoluminescence properties and good biocompatibility, are emerging as promising effective chemical sensors (Guo et al., 2021; Kang & Zhu, 2019). Nonetheless, apart from the scarcity of Au and Ag or other monetary metals, the accuracy and sensitivity with their single emission are often easily interfered with by the microenvironment, and the local luminescent molecules are vulnerable to photobleaching and oxidation (Lee, Kim, & Sessler, 2015; Park, Kwon, Lee, Yoon, & Shin, 2020). And it is necessary to develop an accurate and simple method based on other metal ions/clusters for the pesticide residues sensing. Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) have attracted increasing interest in the fluorescence sensing, bioimaging and therapeutical fields for its charming luminescent merits and low toxicities. (Lai, Wong, & Rogach, 2020). Meanwhile, the ratiometric fluorescent detection system can not only effectively improve stability but also enhance the sensitivity in ensuring the quantification of the binary system, wherein the benchmark can prevent the luminescent center from internal infinitesimal disturbance and outer distraction.

In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system based on N-CQDs@CuNCs complex was constructed for the selective and ultrasensitive detection of thiram and paraquat; blue-emitted nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were selected as the benchmark, and red-emitted CuNCs were used as the luminescent center (Scheme 1). The ratiometric fluorescent probe cannot just detect two kinds of pesticides selectively in different solvent systems. Furthermore, the sensitivity of detection can reach nanomolar level, in line with the standard limits of relevant regulations. Moreover, in addition to fluorescence detection, visual detection is also explored in this research, which can bring more direct signals. This kind of dual-mode detection can show the above merits of both methods at the same time, thus improving the accuracy and diversity of detection. Furthermore, the ratiometric fluorescent probe was applied in the practical detection of thiram within several food samples, which resulted in satisfactory recovery, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescent probe system is expected to be a powerful and efficient tool in the on-site monitoring of the quality and safety of food.

Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O) was obtained from J & K Scientific Ltd. Ethanol was purchased from XiLong SCIENTIFIC. 2,3,5,6-Tetrafluorothiophenol (TFTP, 98%) was purchased from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co., Ltd. Citric acid, ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 30%) and thiram were all purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). Paraquat was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai). All chemicals were used as received without further purification unless otherwise specified. Ultrapure water (≥18 ΜΩ cm) was self-prepared via Millipore and used throughout the whole experiment.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained by employing a transmission electron microscope (FEI Talos F200X, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). The samples were prepared by depositing droplets on Cu grids (200 mesh, coated with carbon film) with the solvent evaporated under ambient conditions. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a DR 6000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (HACH, Colorado, USA). The fluorescence spectra were measured by a Lumina fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). The deionized water was supplied by a Milli-Q water purification system (Merck Millipore, USA). The sample treatment was carried out by using an ultrasonic instrument (5510E-DTH, Branson Ultrasonic Corporation, Danbury, CT 06813, USA) and the Heraeus Multifuge X3R centrifuge (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany). All experiments were carried out at room temperature.

The CuNCs were synthesized according to the procedure from a previously reported paper with minor modifications (Han et al., 2018). Briefly, 500 μL of (100 mM) Cu(NO3)2·3H2O and 49.5 mL of ethanol were mixed, after which 1 mL of TFTP was added into the solution. A yellow turbid solution was formed immediately, and the mixed solution was then incubated at 25 °C in a water bath and gently stirred for 30 min. A yellow precipitate was isolated by centrifugation and washed repeatedly with ethanol. Finally, the product was dispersed in ethanol for subsequent experiments.

N-CQDs were carried out using a previous report with a simple modification (Y. Yang et al., 2020). Specifically, 200 mg citric acid and 200 μL of ammonium hydroxide were mixed and dissolved in 12 mL water with vigorous stirring until the reagents were completely dissolved. Then, the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-sealed autoclave and incubated at 190 °C for 10 h. The stock solution finally turned brown, indicating the formation of N-CQDs. After it was cooled to ambient temperature naturally, the supernatant was centrifuged at 3494×g to remove particles. Finally, the obtained brown solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection for thiram and paraquat
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence detection for thiram was described as follows: 900 μL of CuNCs (130 μg/mL) and 50 μL of N-CQDs (2 mg/mL) were injected into a centrifuge tube. After that, 50 μL of thiram standard solution at different concentrations was mixed with the abovementioned mixtures. The analytical performances of the prepared fluorescence probe were investigated by recording the photoluminescence spectra of CuNCs and N-CQDs. The calibration plots were plotted between the values of the intensity ratio (I620/I425) and the concentration of thiram. The detection system described herein was 95% ethanol since both the CuNCs and thiram were dissolved in ethanol, while the N-CQDs were dissolved in water.

The selectivity of the sensing system was evaluated by the addition of various interfering cations and pesticides, including Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, paraquat, monocrotophos, acephate, phosmet, bromophos-methyl, dimethoate and methidadathion. The excitation wavelength was set as 350 nm for all fluorescence measurements, and the emission slit width was set as 3 nm.

The procedure for the paraquat assay is similar to that for the thiram assay described above, mainly except that the solvent of the CuNCs is replaced with an aqueous solution. Herein, the final detection system for paraquat is 100% water. Besides that, the concentrations of CuNCs and N-CQDs are 217 μg/mL and 4 mg/mL, respectively. And the excitation wavelength was set as 370 nm for the paraquat assay.

Solutions of thiram and paraquat at different concentrations were added to the probe system, and the testing method was the same as the fluorescence detection method mentioned above. Fluorescence color photographs were taken by a HUAWEI p30plus smartphone under a 365 nm and 254 nm UV lamp in a dark box (the ISO value was 500, and the shutter speed was 20/1 s). Relying on the smartphone app “RGB”, the red channel values of samples under 365 nm were read for linear relation calculation.

Three kinds of food (Semen nelumbinis, Coix lacryma-Jobi and ginseng) were chosen to evaluate the feasibility of the as-prepared ratiometric fluorescence probe in real samples. These samples were purchased from a drugstore in Macau, China. The sample treatment procedure was prepared according to a previous study (Asgari et al., 2020; Mei, Shu, Lv, Liu, & Wang, 2020; Zhao et al., 2019). The above samples were first cut into small slices and then mixed thoroughly. Then, 2 g of weighed samples with thiram at different concentrations were mixed with 5 mL of ethanol and extracted for 10 min in an ultrasonic ice-water bath. Then, the obtained solution was centrifuged (3494 × g) to remove the insoluble substance; 4 mL of the supernatant was transferred to another centrifuge tube, evaporated to dryness under a stream of nitrogen and redissolved with ethanol (1 mL) for analysis. Finally, we detected a certain concentration of thiram spiked into samples according to the abovementioned procedures for the recovery study.

To measure the detection performance of the fluorescent complex probe, several photophysical experiments were conducted at ambient temperature. The liquid UV–Vis measurements of the as-synthesized CuNCs and N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 A. The CuNCs in ethanol solution showed characteristic absorption peaks at 263, 325 and 435 nm. The strong absorption peak at 263 nm could be assigned to the K-band absorption of the π-system ligands, and the UV–Vis spectrum of N-CQDs in water solution presents an obvious absorption peak at approximately 329 nm, which could be attributed to n → π* charge transfer in the CO transformation (Lu et al., 2019; Zhou, Zhou, Gong, Zhang, & Li, 2015). As displayed in Fig. 2B, the CuNCs exhibited broad emission at 620 nm when excited at either 340 or 427 nm, and the infrared emission could be due to the cluster-entered (3CC) triplet excited states of the Cu3S2 center (Wang, Li, Zheng, Huang, & Li, 2014). For the N-CQDs, there is an emission peak centered at 427 nm when excited at 340 nm (Fig. 2C), whose emission is dependent on the carbonized exciton Bohr radius and is well in accordance with the radii of these as-prepared carbon dots (Y. Yang et al., 2020).

The phase stability of the target luminescent CuNCs moiety was investigated by replacing ethanol with a water solution in the designed proportion. As shown in Fig. S2, when the ethanol fraction reached ∼20%, the emission peak position remained almost the same, with a ∼40% decrease in fluorescence intensity. When the water fraction reached 100%, which formed the CuNCs water solution, the fluorescence intensity decreased to 67% of the original. It can be concluded that water can serve as a good solvent, while ethanol works as a poor solvent, thus leading to aggregation-induced emission (AIE) behavior (Luo et al., 2001). With the increase in ethanol, the intramolecular movement is restricted to some extent, which leads to the enhancement of fluorescence. This phenomenon also showed that the CuNCs were stable enough to maintain their characteristic red emission in both ethanol or water solvents and their mixed solvents, which further promoted the convenience of practical detection in thiram and paraquat.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL mechanism of the ratiometric nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, a ratiometric nanoprobe was constructed by mixing the as-synthesized CuNCs and N-CQDs in an optimal proportion. Optimization of excitation wavelength is shown in Fig. S3A, the highest fluorescence intensity of CuNCs was achieved under 350 nm excitation in 95% ethanol solution. In the aqueous system (Fig. S3B), optimal excitation wavelength was selected as 370 nm since both CuNCs and N-CQD exhibit strong fluorescence under the irradiations. As shown in Fig. 3 A, upon excitation at 350 nm, a single emission peak at 620 nm of CuNCs can be observed, as well as the emission at 425 nm of N-CQDs. Furthermore, when mixed together, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs located at 425 nm decreased by approximately 50%, indicating that there may be a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process between the two materials. For verification, as shown in Fig. 3B, there is a broad overlap between the N-CQDs emission spectrum and the CuNCs absorption spectrum, indicating that the excited state of N-CQDs can be effectively transferred to the ground state of CuNCs (Qian, Cui, et al., 2020). The fluorescence lifetimes of CuNCs and N-CQDs can also provide evidence of the FRET process. As depicted in Fig. S4 and Table S1, the average fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs shifted to 6.67 ns from 8.10 ns in the presence of CuNCs, and the lifetime of CuNCs changed from 0.32 ns to 1.33 ns. It can be inferred that the CuNCs act as energy acceptors, while the N-CQDs work as energy donors in the typical FRET process (Medintz et al., 2003).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence assay for thiram and paraquat
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the possibility of this ratiometric fluorescence probe in the rapid detection of targeted pesticides, the fluorescence signal of the probe was recorded upon injection of various concentrations of thiram and paraquat. As observed in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity at 620 nm of the CuNCs decreased continuously with increasing thiram content, while the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm of N-CQDs remained invariable. These phenomena indicated that the concentration of thiram only affected the fluorescence intensity of the CuNCs and caused little effect on the N-CQDs. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of thiram was constructed successfully with a good linear correlation over the concentration range from 10 to 500 nM, and the regression equation was I0/I = 0.00301C + 1.03746 (I0 and I refer to the ratio of the fluorescence intensity I620/I425 in the absence and presence of thiram, respectively; C is the concentration of thiram, nM) with a correlation coefficient (R) of 0.99892. Using the 3σ rule (Thompson, Ellison, & Wood, 2002) (σ = S0/S, S0 is known as the standard deviation of the blank after repeated measurements, and S is the slope of the calibration curve), the limit of detection (LOD) for thiram was calculated to be approximately 7.49 nM (0.02 mg/kg) (Fig. 4B), which is much lower than the threshold residue limits for thiram (0.3 mg/kg) in ginseng permitted by the national food safety standard (GB 2763-2019) and the recommended quality standards of thiram (7 ppm) by the environmental protection agency of the USA (Chu et al., 2020). Moreover, compared with other proposed methods for thiram detection, the detection performance is comparable or even superior to other works (Table S2).

Since paraquat is water soluble, it may be possible to detect this toxic pesticide more sensitively in solution systems with higher water fractions. Therefore, we changed the whole detection solvent from 95% ethanol to 100% water, and the detection sensitivity of paraquat was obtained as expected. Paraquat with gradient concentrations varying from 0 to 200 nM was added to the N-CQDs@CuNCs detection system, and the changes in fluorescence intensity were recorded at ambient temperature (Fig. 4C). The changes in fluorescence intensity were similar to those seen in thiram. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs remained constant, while the fluorescence intensity of the CuNCs decreased continuously with the incremental addition of paraquat. To clarify the relationship and evaluate the amount of paraquat, a good linear correlation between I0/I and the concentration of paraquat was established with the following equation: I0/I = 0.01826x + 1.01153 (R2 = 0.98752). The linear detection range is from 5 to 100 nM (Fig. 4D). The LOD for paraquat calculated by the 3σ rule was found to be as low as 3.03 nM, which is superior to previously documented methods (Table S2).

In order to verify the reproducibility of the method, we tested the fluorescence intensity of the probe with and without thiram (paraquat) in three different days (n = 3). As shown in the Fig. S5, there was no significant change in fluorescence intensity either the probe itself or after the addition of the tested substance. Therefore, the detection system has reliable reproducibility and meets the detection requirements.

In addition, we used a smartphone to record the color changes generated by the interactions between the two pesticides at different concentrations and the probe under an ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 4E). Thiram and paraquat solutions with higher concentrations have shown distinct visual shocks, which can be applied in the direct identification of pesticide varieties. For thiram, the original pink turned dark cyan upon excitation at 365 nm and changed to brown from the initial deep yellow upon irradiation at 254 nm. For paraquat, the higher concentration leads to deep blue and light brown excitation at 365 nm and 254 nm, respectively. Moreover, according to the analysis of red channel values extracted from the RGB results, linear relationships were obtained in the centration range of 0.05–50 μM for thiram with R2 = 0.987 and 0.01–5 μM for paraquat with R2 = 0.973 (Fig. S6). Conclusively, the as-prepared fluorescent probe showed promising sensing merits in qualitative and quantitative analysis of paraquat and thiram.

The selectivity of this probe system was evaluated by several parallel measurements in recording the fluorescence response against other contaminative pesticides and ions. When the detection system consisted of 95% ethanol and 5% water, the detection performance of this probe against pesticides is listed in Fig. 5 A. The influence of most additive interferents is negligible except thiram and paraquat, and thiram seems to have a more sensitive impact than paraquat. The results showed that the N-CQDs@CuNCs probe system was highly efficient and specific to thiram in an ethanol-dominated environment. When the detection system was transformed into 100% water, as depicted in Fig. 5B, almost nothing but paraquat showed a positive response, indicating that high selectivity of paraquat was achieved in a water-dominated environment. These results indicate that the N-CQDs@CuNCs probe system can realize selective ratiometric detection of thiram and paraquat, which holds a promising monitor for evaluating the safe quality of thiram- and paraquat-polluted food.

To determine the sensing mechanism, PL spectra, UV–Vis spectra and zeta potential measurements were combined to investigate the possible interaction between these analytes and the fluorescent probe. As displayed in the PL spectra in Fig. S7, the addition of thiram and paraquat had no positive effects on the fluorescence intensity of individual N-CQDs, suggesting that fluorescence quenching was independent of N-CQDs. As plotted in Fig. S8, the UV absorption peak of the N-CQDs@CuNCs was slightly changed, and no new peaks appeared after the addition of thiram and paraquat, indicating that no new complex formed after the addition of the analytes in the probe system. The fluorescence lifetimes were also determined and are shown in Fig. S9. The addition of these analytes resulted in no significant lifetime change, suggesting that no FRET process occurred in the luminescence quenching behavior. Furthermore, zeta potential measurements were carried out, and the results are enumerated in Fig. S10 and Table S3. It can clearly be seen that the average potential values of the N-CQDs@CuNCs complex probe increased from −35.2 mV to 30.3 mV after the addition of paraquat. The value shifted from −451 mV to −299 mV after the addition of thiram . Therefore, it can be speculated that the fluorescence quenching may be caused by electrostatic interactions (Bhamore et al., 2016). It is well-known that paraquat belongs to a category of cationic viologens with a positive charge (Ren et al., 2021). Latest research was reported to monitor paraquat by using negatively charged molecules (Zhao, Zhang, & Zhang, 2018). The fluorescent probe of N-CQDs@CuNCs in this research has been confirmed to be negatively charged (Table S3), so presumably paraquat would accept electrons from the anionic complex and be capable of quenching the fluorescence. As for thiram, there are some other studies have pointed out that copper ions can easily break the SS bond of thiram and form Cu2+-thiram complex, leading to fluorescence quenching, which may also explain the behavior in this research (Cheng et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2018).

To evaluate the applicability of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence probe for real samples, recovery tests were conducted by spiking thiram into several plant products that are homologous to medicine and food, such as semen nelumbinis, coix lacryma-jobi and ginseng samples. A summary of the determined concentrations of thiram in food is shown in Table S4. The analyst recovery rates based on luminescence spectrometry were found to be 82%–114%, in line with the requirement of 70%–120% for the determination of pesticide residues in the recently released Chinese Pharmacopoeia, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescence probe could reliably and effectively detect thiram in real samples.

In conclusion, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system based on N-CQDs and copper nanoclusters was constructed for the quantitative analysis of quality safety in food. This fluorescent probe shows the selective and ultrasensitive detection of thiram and paraquat with recording-breaking detection limits of 7.49 nM and 3.03 nM, respectively. In the meantime, the ratiometric fluorescence probe has gained satisfactory recovery results in the range of 82%–114% for practical food measurements.

Ling Chen: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jian Lu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Mai Luo: Formal analysis, Software, Visualization. Hua Yu: Project administration. Xiaojia Chen: Formal analysis, Software. Jiagang Deng: Funding acquisition. Xiaotao Hou: Resources. Erwei Hao: Resources. Jinchao Wei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Peng Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Macau Science and Technology Development Fund (File No. 147/2019/A3 and 0047/2020/AGJ), Guangxi Innovation-driven Development Special Foundation Project (File No. GuiKe AA18118049) and Research Committee of the University of Macau (File No. MYRG2018-00239-ICMS).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.132139.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Asgari et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanofibrillar cellulose/Au@Ag nanoparticle nanocomposite as a SERS substrate for detection of paraquat and thiram in lettuce
Microchimica Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Asgari, S., Sun, L., Lin, J., Weng, Z. Y., Wu, G. F., Zhang, Y., & Lin, M. S. (2020). Nanofibrillar cellulose/Au@Ag nanoparticle nanocomposite as a SERS substrate for detection of paraquat and thiram in lettuce. Microchimica Acta, 187(7). 10.1007/s00604-020-04358-9.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bhamore et al., 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step green synthetic approach for the preparation of multicolor emitting copper nanoclusters and their applications in chemical species sensing and bioimaging
Biosensors and Bioelectronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bhamore, J. R., Jha, S., Mungara, A. K., Singhal, R. K., Sonkeshariya, D., & Kailasa, S. K. (2016). One-step green synthetic approach for the preparation of multicolor emitting copper nanoclusters and their applications in chemical species sensing and bioimaging. Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 80, 243-248. 10.1016/j.bios.2016.01.066.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Botta et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 3D structured laser engraves decorated with gold nanoparticle SERS chips for paraquat herbicide detection in environments
Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Botta, R., Eiamchai, P., Horprathum, M., Limwichean, S., Chananonnawathorn, C., Patthanasettakul, V., . . . Nuntawong, N. (2020). 3D structured laser engraves decorated with gold nanoparticle SERS chips for paraquat herbicide detection in environments. Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 304, 127327. 10.1016/j.snb.2019.127327.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Cheng et al., 2022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Copper ions assisted fluorescent detection of some dithiocarbamates based on nickel nanocluster with aggregation-induced emission enhancement behavior
Journal of Hazardous Materials
